### Dominic 


:::: {layout="[60,40]"}
::: {}
0) Title
1) Abstract
2) Introduction
:::
Write these last, find out what the report becomes. Then write Introduction etc
::::
3) Description of our thoughts & work; main source: our notes
4) reflection on the work
5) conclusion 


**Starting with 3):**

- our background, because this greatly affects how we could come to conclusions:

The two of us have a similar interest in physics. We studied similar courses with interests in the abstract mathematical concepts.

- a description of our work throughout the two weeks

We, two people "A" and "D", were provided with an atomic physics experiment which allows students to perform the an experiment called "electromagnetically induced transparency". 
Within this framework the question that we must face is "What is it that we want to understand?"
Before we could tackle the task together, we needed a platform that allowed for independant working and thinking and also to unify our thoughts.
The platform we chose was github.
As a new working environment it took some initial time to set it up and to get it started and to adjust to the workflow this platform provided. 

- planning of repository structure
- basic workflow
- branches
- merging and merge conflicts

We decided for the following structure:

```
data/
  orientation.json 
figures/
notes/
  pictures/
  sections/
  lab_notes.qmd
  awais.qmd
  dominic.qmd
report/
```

With the goal of to have a structure to allow for constructive collaboration of two people. 
The "data" directory is a location for all data, with "orientation.json" capable for keeping track of the potential filenames (as keys) and the metadata. The notes contain the lab notes.
With a folder for pictures and sub sections that can be included. The two files "awais.qmd" and "dominic.qmd" were for seperate working, with the idea of concluding and merging our common knowladge in "lab_notes.qmd".

We went about this topic by first looking at the components of our experiment: 

- laser physics: external-cavity diode-laser (ECDL)
- atomic physics: $^{87}\text{Rn}(I=3/2)$, $^{85}\text{Rn}(I=5/2)$
- optics: alignment and optical components
    - linear polarizer
    - quarter wave plate
    - half wave plate
    - lense
    - Fabry-Pérot interferometer
- spectroscopy techniques: Absorption, Flourescence, and Saturation
- interferometery: Fabry-Pérot interferometer

From these aspects that we identified and thoroughly discussed, we "decided upon" the detailed study of the "external-cavity diode-laser". It was the goal to get an understanding that so that we could setup a numerical simulation of the ECDL. The process of deciding was based on:

- a discussion on each of the parts 
- arguments of us describing our interests
- a written document that 

After deciding upon a topic
